caption: Figure 1. a) Phase diagram, demonstrating liquid $( \mathsf { L } ) , \mathsf { \& } \gamma ,$ and κ-carbide formations in low density steel system. b) The phase fraction variations, showing the occurrence of an order-disordered transition from δ to B2 at 1125 °C. c,d) The equilibrium distributions of solutes, showing elemental partitioning from γ-matrix to B2. The outputs are consistent with Ref. [15] for the same alloy composition.
paper_abstract: The article involves synthesis, characterization, and mechanical property studies of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% steel, prepared by hot rolling, cold rolling (CR), and annealing treatment. A partial substitution of iron (density, 7.8 g/cc) with a lighter element like Al (density, 2.7 g/cc) is strategic to fabricate the low density steel, following a thumb rule that 1 wt% Al additions in steel lead to the density reduction of roughly 1.2%. However, the sample processing imposes challenges, based on the microstructure that an excessive use of Al in the alloy chemistry results in 27% δ-ferrite during solidification-casting. The hot and CR transforms the δ into B2 stringer bands, that is, an Fe-Al-based intermetallic phase. A bamboo-like morphology of the B2-stringer after heat treatment causes a premature failure of the austenitic steel. With proper process control, the article finds a way to circumvent the issue. The outcome results in nearly 1 GPa yield strength, 28% total elongation, and over 1.7 kJ mol1 (or 238.4 J cm3 ) toughness, tantamount to the development of crack-resistant ultrahigh strength steel for the automotive body in white. The current article also analyzes intra- and intergranular precipitation of the B2 second-phase, tailoring the mechanical properties.
paper_title: Microstructural Modulation through Process Control Leading to Ultrastrong Ductile Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% Low-Density Steel
citation_sentences: The experimental data were consolidated with thermodynamic calculations using the 7 database of - . The TCFE Thermo Calcphase diagram was calculated as a pseudo-binary for the multicomponent system, showing the onset of liquid to δ-ferrite, peritectic, and eutectoid reactions at different temperatures, a. A change in the volume fraction of phases with tem-Figure 1peratures has been plotted in Figure 1b, indicating a reduction in δ-ferrite fractions on its way to B2 formation until the eutectoid reaction at $8 5 1 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ The extension of eutectoid transformation near annealing temperature $( 9 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ enables a fresh precipitation of B2 by reducing the γ phase fractions (Figure 1b). The γ to κ-carbide precipitation is limited in the system (Figure 1b). The thermodynamic calculations in Figure 1c,d further state that the solute-partitioning from γ enriches the alloying elements into B2 through a local equilibrium at the phase interface.

The as-cast solidified microstructure has been demonstrated in a. The dendrite fractions appear to be significant, due to Figure 2a high concentration of Al as a ferrite stabilizer alloyed in the composition. The δ-ferrite nucleates directly from the liquid phase at a high temperature (Figure 1a). During the next stage, the liquid and δ-ferrite combine to form γ by peritectic reaction (Figure 1a).[2] It is most likely that the thickening of γ-solid at liquid/δ-ferrite phase interfaces blocks the solute exchange, thereby causing cessation of δ-ferrite decomposition by incomplete peritectic reaction.[2] Evidence is provided by 27% δ-ferrite remaining in the as-cast sample microstructure at room temperature by maintaining an equilibrium with γ-phase. The solutepartition into δ-ferrite from γ-matrix during homogenization treatment at 1150 °C for 2 h can transform the δ to B2 through order-disordered transition prevailing at 1125 °C in this alloy system (Figure 1b–d).[1,15] The solute-enrichment makes the dendrites strong.[12] As a result, the present work intends to perform HR to a higher degree and followed by a minimum CR at room temperature, restoring metal sheets free from cracking.

The execution of HR between 1050 and 900 °C transforms the dendrites into lamellar stringer bands, spanning over multiple austenite grains (Figure 2b). During the hot deformation, the aspect ratio of dendrites decreases in the normal direction by channeling mass in RD. In order to synchronize plastic flow, the γ-matrix also undergoes rigorous plastic deformation. The localization of strain in γ has been tracked with shear band formations inside the matrix phase (Figure 2d). Fine-scale B2 tends to nucleate at those shear bands, alleviating lattice strain. The repeated HR in nine stages causes shape deformation and a partial dissolution of dendrites, in accordance with the equilibrium diagram (Figure 1b). A rough estimation with - software Image Jreveals the reduction of dendrite fractions from 27% in the ascast microstructure to 19% after HR. In the next stage, the CR causes panacking of γ, a further thinning of B2 stringer bands, and a shape deformation of precipitated IG-B2 (Figure 2c). The lamellar shape morphology of B2 stringer bands through a repeated thickness reduction is an interesting observation pertaining to enhanced plasticity by an intermetallic phase shown in this work.[12] The insight demonstrates a future hope that the low density steel can be flattened and rolled with conventional metallurgy applied in commercial lines, leading to the fabrication of the bulk sample.

A bright-field transmission electron micrograph of the longer annealed A30 in a reveals the microstructural informa-Figure 4tion in finer details in association with the previous FE-SEM images. The GB-B2 appears to be irregular in shape, nucleating along prior austenite grain boundaries. The IG-B2 is needleshaped, and it nucleates mainly inside URE-γ, being left out in smaller numbers after the annealing treatment (Figure 3d and 4a). A specific reason for the presence of URE-γ is the B2 stringer bands, surrounding unrecrystallized grains in some regions of the sample microstructure (Figure 3d–f ). The B2 delays the recrystallization of γ, similar to a carbide, nitride, or carbonitride, controlling rate kinetics in different steels without dissolution.[17,18] The heat treatment releases residual stress by nucleating annealing twins sporadically inside the γ grains (Figure 4a). The twinned plates and boundaries were the heterogeneous nucleation sites for B2 precipitation, as shown in the inset of Figure 4a. The TEMenergy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analysis in Figure 4b–f reveals that the B2 is enriched in Fe, Mn, Al, and Ni, denoting the chemical formula to be Fe(Ni, Mn)Al. The solute partitioning from γ during homogenization and annealing treatment enriches those elements into B2, since volume diffusion of the substitutional elements may not be easy down to the annealing temperatures (Figure 1c,d). A negligible solid solubility of carbon in B2 confirms the precipitated phase as an intermetallic (Figure 1d and 4d). Interestingly, although Ni is a well-known γ-stabilizer, it is enriched in B2, without local-─ ─ izing in its designated place, γ (Figure 1c,d). The unusual trend─ ─ lies in the fact that the Ni-partition into B2 replaces some of the Fe Al with Ni Al bonds. The atomic scale ordering renders the B2 more stable, as Ni Al is stronger than the Fe Al bond.[19–21] A stronger atomic bond, making the B2 nonshearable, is often behind the active consideration of IG-B2 for precipitation hardening in the previous work.[12] The dislocation looping around B2 particles induces precipitation strengthening by the Ashby– Orowan equation[15,22,23]

caption: Figure 2. a) Optical images, showing (a) δ-ferrite in austenite (γ) matrix of the as-cast sample, and the transformation of δ-ferrite into lamellar B2 stringer bands after b) HR and c) CR. d) The FESEM image, showing intragranular-B2 (IG-B2) precipitation at shear bands of HR-γ.
paper_abstract: The article involves synthesis, characterization, and mechanical property studies of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% steel, prepared by hot rolling, cold rolling (CR), and annealing treatment. A partial substitution of iron (density, 7.8 g/cc) with a lighter element like Al (density, 2.7 g/cc) is strategic to fabricate the low density steel, following a thumb rule that 1 wt% Al additions in steel lead to the density reduction of roughly 1.2%. However, the sample processing imposes challenges, based on the microstructure that an excessive use of Al in the alloy chemistry results in 27% δ-ferrite during solidification-casting. The hot and CR transforms the δ into B2 stringer bands, that is, an Fe-Al-based intermetallic phase. A bamboo-like morphology of the B2-stringer after heat treatment causes a premature failure of the austenitic steel. With proper process control, the article finds a way to circumvent the issue. The outcome results in nearly 1 GPa yield strength, 28% total elongation, and over 1.7 kJ mol1 (or 238.4 J cm3 ) toughness, tantamount to the development of crack-resistant ultrahigh strength steel for the automotive body in white. The current article also analyzes intra- and intergranular precipitation of the B2 second-phase, tailoring the mechanical properties.

citation_sentences: The cold working causes intense metal sheet deformation. The restoration desires annealing treatment, performed at 900 °C for 5–30 min. The representative sample microstructures are given in  , revealing intra-(IG-B2) and intergranular (GB-B2) Figure 3precipitation of B2 second phase in this stage, additionally. The IG-B2 nucleates within unrecrystallized-γ (URE-γ), containing shear bands at large for their heterogeneous nucleation (Figure 3a–f ). The precipitation of IG-B2 at the grain body of fully recrystallized-γ (RE-γ) was negligible, given by the fact that intragranular precipitation of second-phase particles with a body centered cubic (BCC) crystal structure in face centered cubic (FCC)γ was difficult through high coherency strain or activation energy barrier involved in the solid-state precipitation reaction (Figure 3g).[16] The IG-B2 is finer in A5, and it tends to grow bigger into a needle shape through mutual coalescence in shear bands during annealing for 30 min (Figure 3d–f ). The GB-B2 is coarse, due to enhanced solute flux from the grain boundary

regions (Figure 3g–i). The grain size of RE-γ increases with the annealing time. The URE-γ grains were coarse and elongated along prior RD by maintaining a pancake structure despite the annealing (Figure 3a–c). Nevertheless, the IG-B2, GB-B2, and B2 stringer bands were the three different microstructural morphologies of B2, retained after the annealing treatment.

A bright-field transmission electron micrograph of the longer annealed A30 in a reveals the microstructural informa-Figure 4tion in finer details in association with the previous FE-SEM images. The GB-B2 appears to be irregular in shape, nucleating along prior austenite grain boundaries. The IG-B2 is needleshaped, and it nucleates mainly inside URE-γ, being left out in smaller numbers after the annealing treatment (Figure 3d and 4a). A specific reason for the presence of URE-γ is the B2 stringer bands, surrounding unrecrystallized grains in some regions of the sample microstructure (Figure 3d–f ). The B2 delays the recrystallization of γ, similar to a carbide, nitride, or carbonitride, controlling rate kinetics in different steels without dissolution.[17,18] The heat treatment releases residual stress by nucleating annealing twins sporadically inside the γ grains (Figure 4a). The twinned plates and boundaries were the heterogeneous nucleation sites for B2 precipitation, as shown in the inset of Figure 4a. The TEMenergy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analysis in Figure 4b–f reveals that the B2 is enriched in Fe, Mn, Al, and Ni, denoting the chemical formula to be Fe(Ni, Mn)Al. The solute partitioning from γ during homogenization and annealing treatment enriches those elements into B2, since volume diffusion of the substitutional elements may not be easy down to the annealing temperatures (Figure 1c,d). A negligible solid solubility of carbon in B2 confirms the precipitated phase as an intermetallic (Figure 1d and 4d). Interestingly, although Ni is a well-known γ-stabilizer, it is enriched in B2, without local-─ ─ izing in its designated place, γ (Figure 1c,d). The unusual trend─ ─ lies in the fact that the Ni-partition into B2 replaces some of the Fe Al with Ni Al bonds. The atomic scale ordering renders the B2 more stable, as Ni Al is stronger than the Fe Al bond.[19–21] A stronger atomic bond, making the B2 nonshearable, is often behind the active consideration of IG-B2 for precipitation hardening in the previous work.[12] The dislocation looping around B2 particles induces precipitation strengthening by the Ashby– Orowan equation[15,22,23]

where  is the shear modulus (70 GPa),[24]  is the Burgers vector $\mathrm { o f } \gamma ( 0 . 2 6 \mathrm { n m } ) , ^ { [ 2 4 ] } D _ { \mathrm { I G - B } 2 }$ bis the average size of IG-B2, and $f _ { \mathrm { I G - B } 2 }$ is the fractional distribution of IG-B2. The estimation of $D _ { \mathrm { I G - B } 2 }$ (0.29 μm) and $f _ { \mathrm { I G - B } 2 }$ D(1.1%) within RE-γ (Figure 3a) provided $\sigma _ { \mathrm { { I C } - B 2 } }$ fof 23 MPa. The IG-B2 (0.2 μm) with a net phase fraction of 2.6% in URE-γ provided the $\sigma _ { \mathrm { { I C - B 2 } } }$ of 43 MPa. Thereby, the total IG-B2 within RE-γ and URE-γ provided the precipitation strengthening of 66 MPa. The annealing over a longer duration coarsens IG-B2 and reduces $f _ { \mathrm { I G - B } 2 }$ for the precipitation strengthening to be feven lower. In order to understand the role of B2 stringer bands, the following section analyzes the mechanical properties.

Optical and SEM images, illustrating b,e) stringer band annihilation, c) crack formation, and d) microvoid nucleation at deformed tensile gauge. f ) EBSD phase map, revealing grain boundaries inside B2 stringer bands.

citation_sentences: The engineering stress–strain curves of uniaxial tensile samples processed under HR, CR, and annealed conditions are shown in a. The HR condition exhibits nearly 6.8% duc-Figure 5tility, and CR reduces it even further; thus, adding extra attention to annealed conditions for tensile properties. The incorporation of annealing treatment increases ductility, but the strength– ductility balance varies inconsistently in different annealed conditions. For example, the tensile strength in A15 was higher than that of A10, and the ductility in A30 increased slightly despite a longer annealing. The energy absorbed before fracture, calculated from an integral area under the stress–strain curves, increases moderately as a function of annealing time of the samples (inset, Figure 5a). The unsatisfactory stress–strain response was subjected to verification by performing fracture analysis at the deformed tensile gauge.

As compared to conventional fractography, the microstructure analysis at the crack tip is qualitative (Figure 5b–e). A systematic study reveals that macrovoids nucleate inside B2 stringer bands. As the tensile strain increases, the macrovoid coalescence happens, triggering crack initiation in B2 stringer bands (Figure 5d). If a stringer band is thicker, the crack formation is higher inside the lamellar structure at a regular interval (Figure 5c,e). A key observation on top is the decohesion of a B2 stringer band in segments (Figure 5b). The merger of several microcracks into a bigger crack perpendicular to the load axis and the debonding of stringer band segments in the loading axis lead to biaxial failure of the tensile gauge by ultimately detaching a chunk of material from the deformed tensile gauge (Figure 5b,e). A statistical distribution of thicker stringer bands localized in a region would fast-track the microcrack merger, resulting in a catastrophic failure of an entire tensile gauge (Figure 5b). The escalating matter was thoroughly analyzed with an EBSD phase map in Figure 5f, pinpointing crack initiation at the grain boundaries of B2 stringer bands. A specific reason is that the B2 stringer bands undergo intense prior plastic deformation, and thereafter annealing treatment, leading to static recrystallization into a bamboo-like structure (Figure 5f ). As the B2 stringer is an intermetallic phase, the grain boundary decohesion was inevitable (see Figure 5d–f ). In the next course, the annhilation of a B2 stringer band segment happens, based on the EBSD characterization that the static recrystallization of a B2 stringer band subdivides its polygonized structure into various grains or lamellar segments having different local orientations inherited from the prior plastic deformation, as shown by color mismatch in the orientation map (Figure 5f ). The statistical dominance of a particular color in a stringer band is considered the preferable orientation, leaving behind the minority grains to be debonded as an unpreferred orientation. The stress concentrations within the B2 stringer bands curtail the overall strength or ductility in the samples (Figure 5a).

In order to overcome the flow instability, a conservative idea could be the elimination of CR plus annealing treatment. However, the approach incorporates a poor strength–ductility balance, as is evident in the sole HR sample (Figure 5a). Thus, a process control is rationale for a feasible solution. The execution applies a relatively high speed HR at the rate 10 rpm against a merely 1 rpm in the previous case, reducing the plate thickness from 14  2 mm (slightly more), followed by a 1 mm thickness reduction by CR. With annealing treatment similar to the previous experiments for 5–30 min, the YS increases to 1 GPa in a. The strength–ductility variations Figure 6also become consistent, as less annealed A5 exhibits higher strength, and longer annealed the best ductility, raising the toughness to 1.72 kJ mol1 (or 238 J cm3 ) in A30 (inset, Figure 6a). The overall magnitudes appear to be comparable with TRIP aided steel, having the toughness of nearly 0.5–1.5 kJ mol1 or $6 9 { - } 2 0 7 . 8 \mathrm { J } \mathrm { c m } ^ { - 3 } . ^ { [ 1 4 , 2 5 ] }$ The valuable contribution of microstructural modifications was attributed to the refinement of B2 stringer bands, bringing softer γ to the prominence by reducing the stringer band fractions from 16% to 11% in the sample microstructures. The enhanced austenite fraction in between the thinner stringer bands delays the crack propagation and stringer band decohesion, by absorbing the plastic strain preferentially (Figure 6c–f ). The residual dislocations left behind by high-speed HR may increase the YS of A5 to a higher level, as compared to longer annealed samples, annihilating the dislocation density significantly.

The strain partitioning between softer γ and harder B2 plays an important role in strain hardening, analyzed with Holloman’s plot to be in two stages, Figure 6b. The interpretation is based on the calculation of strain hardening exponent ( ) from true stress (σ)–true strain (ε) curves using the equation $\sigma = k \varepsilon ^ { n }$ in logarithkmatic form, where is the strength coefficient. The value k n-obtained from the best linear fits of lnσ versus lnε is initially low, due to yield point elongation (YPE), followed by a steady rise through a combined plastic deformation of γ and B2. The EBSD phase map, along with the Kernel average misorientation (KAM) map, proportional to dislocation density,[26] in a,b reveals Figure 7that both γ and B2 have sufficient capacity to undergo plastic deformation through a shape deformation. Since B2 is a harder phase, the strain concentration is higher in γ succumbing to more deformation (Figure 7b). The observation was compounded in the TEM investigation of A15 after an interrupted tensile test at engineering strain = 0.05, finding a higher disloecation density in γ than that in the B2, Figure 7c. The strain incompatibility between the phases results in YPE. Since B2 is an intermetallic and γ is solute-enriched, the magnitude of YPE is weak during the tensile deformation at the onset of yielding (Figure 5a and 6a). As the tensile deformation progresses, the dislocations released from softer γ tend to get localized around GB-B2 at the phase interfaces near γ grain boundaries, Figure 7d,e. The preferential localization of strain reduces the geometrically necessary dislocation density (GND) in bulk, containing 64% γ. As a result, the (γ  B2) phase mixture reduces the magnitudes of the overall strain hardening exponent to 0.19–0.27 by a rule of mixture (Figure 6b). In order to augment -values beyond the current range, a uniform distribution of nGND inside γ is essential.[23] The current microstructural modulation rules out that possibility with 16% GB-B2 and 3.7% IG-B2, rather nonshearable in nature, blocking the mean-free paths of dislocations (Figure 7e).

caption: Figure 6. a) Engineering stress–strain curves of the samples processed under modified HR, CR, and annealing for 5–30 mins. b) Holloman’s plot, demonstrating the strain hardening exponent (n) for various samples. Optical micrographs of the deformed tensile gauge, showing reduced crack susceptibility in the modified c) A5, d) A15, and e) A30.

The execution applies a relatively high speed HR at the rate 10 rpm against a merely 1 rpm in the previous case, reducing the plate thickness from 14  2 mm (slightly more), followed by a 1 mm thickness reduction by CR. With annealing treatment similar to the previous experiments for 5–30 min, the YS increases to 1 GPa in a. The strength–ductility variations Figure 6also become consistent, as less annealed A5 exhibits higher strength, and longer annealed the best ductility, raising the toughness to 1.72 kJ mol1 (or 238 J cm3 ) in A30 (inset, Figure 6a). The overall magnitudes appear to be comparable with TRIP aided steel, having the toughness of nearly 0.5–1.5 kJ mol1 or $6 9 { - } 2 0 7 . 8 \mathrm { J } \mathrm { c m } ^ { - 3 } . ^ { [ 1 4 , 2 5 ] }$ The valuable contribution of microstructural modifications was attributed to the refinement of B2 stringer bands, bringing softer γ to the prominence by reducing the stringer band fractions from 16% to 11% in the sample microstructures. The enhanced austenite fraction in between the thinner stringer bands delays the crack propagation and stringer band decohesion, by absorbing the plastic strain preferentially (Figure 6c–f ). The residual dislocations left behind by high-speed HR may increase the YS of A5 to a higher level, as compared to longer annealed samples, annihilating the dislocation density significantly.

The strain partitioning between softer γ and harder B2 plays an important role in strain hardening, analyzed with Holloman’s plot to be in two stages, Figure 6b. The interpretation is based on the calculation of strain hardening exponent ( ) from true stress (σ)–true strain (ε) curves using the equation $\sigma = k \varepsilon ^ { n }$ in logarithkmatic form, where is the strength coefficient. The value k n-obtained from the best linear fits of lnσ versus lnε is initially low, due to yield point elongation (YPE), followed by a steady rise through a combined plastic deformation of γ and B2. The EBSD phase map, along with the Kernel average misorientation (KAM) map, proportional to dislocation density,[26] in a,b reveals Figure 7that both γ and B2 have sufficient capacity to undergo plastic deformation through a shape deformation. Since B2 is a harder phase, the strain concentration is higher in γ succumbing to more deformation (Figure 7b). The observation was compounded in the TEM investigation of A15 after an interrupted tensile test at engineering strain = 0.05, finding a higher disloecation density in γ than that in the B2, Figure 7c. The strain incompatibility between the phases results in YPE. Since B2 is an intermetallic and γ is solute-enriched, the magnitude of YPE is weak during the tensile deformation at the onset of yielding (Figure 5a and 6a). As the tensile deformation progresses, the dislocations released from softer γ tend to get localized around GB-B2 at the phase interfaces near γ grain boundaries, Figure 7d,e. The preferential localization of strain reduces the geometrically necessary dislocation density (GND) in bulk, containing 64% γ. As a result, the (γ  B2) phase mixture reduces the magnitudes of the overall strain hardening exponent to 0.19–0.27 by a rule of mixture (Figure 6b). In order to augment -values beyond the current range, a uniform distribution of nGND inside γ is essential.[23] The current microstructural modulation rules out that possibility with 16% GB-B2 and 3.7% IG-B2, rather nonshearable in nature, blocking the mean-free paths of dislocations (Figure 7e).

caption: Figure 7. (a) EBSD phase map, showing co-deformation of B2 stringer bands and $\gamma ,$ and b) a parallel EBSD KAM map, showing strain localization in the respective phases during CR. c) TEM bright field image post-interrupted tensile at plastic strain e = 0.05, showing deformed γ and B2 in A15. d,e) EBSD phase and KAM maps of A15 post-interrupted tensile at e = 0.05, showing strain localization at γ/B2 interface.
paper_abstract: The article involves synthesis, characterization, and mechanical property studies of Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% steel, prepared by hot rolling, cold rolling (CR), and annealing treatment. A partial substitution of iron (density, 7.8 g/cc) with a lighter element like Al (density, 2.7 g/cc) is strategic to fabricate the low density steel, following a thumb rule that 1 wt% Al additions in steel lead to the density reduction of roughly 1.2%. However, the sample processing imposes challenges, based on the microstructure that an excessive use of Al in the alloy chemistry results in 27% δ-ferrite during solidification-casting. The hot and CR transforms the δ into B2 stringer bands, that is, an Fe-Al-based intermetallic phase. A bamboo-like morphology of the B2-stringer after heat treatment causes a premature failure of the austenitic steel. With proper process control, the article finds a way to circumvent the issue. The outcome results in nearly 1 GPa yield strength, 28% total elongation, and over 1.7 kJ mol1 (or 238.4 J cm3 ) toughness, tantamount to the development of crack-resistant ultrahigh strength steel for the automotive body in white. The current article also analyzes intra- and intergranular precipitation of the B2 second-phase, tailoring the mechanical properties.
paper_title: Microstructural Modulation through Process Control Leading to Ultrastrong Ductile Fe–10Al–15Mn–0.8C–5Ni wt% Low-Density Steel
